perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean any information furnished to the Receiving Party or its Representatives, whether oral, written or visual, and regardless of the manner in which it is furnished, by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party or its Representative, and shall include , without limitation: a) information constituting a "Trade S ecret", as defined in Idaho Code, Title 48, Chapter 8; Idaho Trade Secrets Act, as amended from time to time, and will include technical or non -technical data, a formal, a pattern, a compilation, a program, a software program, a device, a method, a technique, a drawing, a process, financial data, financial plans, product plans, non -public forecasts, studies, projections, analyses, all customer data of any kind, or a list of actual or potential customers or suppliers, business and contractual relationships, or any information similar to the foregoing which (i) derives economic value, ac tual or potential, from not being generally known and not readily ascertainable by proper means to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (ii) is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to ma intain its secrecy.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean any information furnished to the Receiving Party or its Representatives, whether oral, written or visual, and regardless of the manner in which it is furnished, by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party or its Representative, and shall include , without limitation: a) information constituting a "Trade S ecret", which, contrary to Idaho Code, Title 48, Chapter 8; Idaho Trade Secrets Act, as amended from time to time, will exclude customer lists and financial data, and will only include technical data directly related to the Build-to-Print Goods.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text directly contradicts the definition of "Trade Secret" under Idaho law by explicitly excluding customer lists and financial data, which are included in the statutory definition. This creates uncertainty about what information is protected under the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Idaho Code § 48-801 defines 'Trade Secret' to include a wide range of information, including customer lists and financial data if they meet certain criteria (economic value from not being generally known, and subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy). The modified contract language explicitly excludes these, directly contradicting the statute's inclusive definition.

Search Idaho Statutes Search Statutes Search Constitution pecnv.out TITLE 48 MONOPOLIES AND TRADE PRACTICES CHAPTER 8 IDAHO TRADE SECRETS ACT 48-801. Definitions. As used in this chapter unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquire

As used in this chapter unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts Idaho Code § 48-801 regarding the definition of a 'Trade Secret'. The original text incorporates the Idaho Trade Secrets Act definition by reference. The changed text explicitly excludes 'customer lists and financial data' from the definition of 'Trade Secret', which are included in the statutory definition if they derive economic value from not being generally known and are subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The scraped law snippet 2, 'Idaho Code Section 48-801 (2022) - DEFINITIONS', confirms that the Idaho Trade Secrets Act defines trade secrets. By explicitly excluding elements included in the statutory definition, the contract creates a direct conflict, making it likely to fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party specifically acknowledges that use or disclosure of the Confidential Information may be subject to U.S. Government’s export laws and regulatio ns, included but not limited to, the International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR) and the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) and warrants that such Party shall make no use or disclosure of the Confidential Information contrary to such laws or othe r applicable laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that use or disclosure of the Confidential Information is not subject to U.S. Government’s export laws and regulatio ns, including the International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR) and the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), and warrants that such Party shall make no use or disclosure of the Confidential Information contrary to applicable laws.
perturbation.explanation: This change states that export laws such as ITAR and EAR do not apply. This is a direct contradiction if the confidential information is technical data related to defense articles or dual-use technologies, which are subject to these regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: 22 U.S.C. § 2778 controls the export and import of defense articles and services. If the 'Confidential Information' includes technical data related to items on the United States Munitions List, it is subject to ITAR. The modified contract language stating ITAR does not apply directly contradicts this law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 22 U.S. Code § 2778 - Control of arms exports and imports | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 22 U.S. Code § 2778 - Control of arms exports and imports U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Presidential control of exports and imports of defense articles and services, guidance of policy, etc.; designation of United States Munitions List; issuance of export licenses; negotiations information (1) In furtherance of world peace and the security and foreign policy of the United States, the President is authorized to control the import and the export of defense articles and defense services and to provide foreign policy guidance to persons of the United States involved in the export and import of such articles and services. The President is authorized to designate those items which shall be considered as defense articles and defense services for the purposes of this section and to promulgate regulations for the import and export of such articles and services. The items so designated shall constitute the United States Munitions List. (2) Decisions on issuing export licenses under this section shall take into account whether the export of an article would contribute to an arms race, aid in the development of weapons of mass destruction , support international terrorism, increase the possibility of outbreak or escalation of conflict, or prejudice the development of bilateral or multilateral arms control or nonproliferation agreements or other arrangements. (3) In exercising the authorities conferred by this section, the President may require that any defense article or defense service be sold under this chapter as a condition of its eligibility for export, and may require that persons engaged in the negotiation for the export of defense articles and services keep the President fully and currently informed of the progress and future prospects of such negotiations. (b) Regi
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly states that 'Confidential Information is not subject to U.S. Government’s export laws and regulations, including the International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR) and the Export Administration Regulations (EAR).' This directly contradicts 22 U.S.C. § 2778, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which authorizes the President to control the import and export of defense articles and services and to designate items considered defense articles under the United States Munitions List. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation accurately state that if the 'Confidential Information' includes technical data related to items on the United States Munitions List, it is subject to ITAR. By stating ITAR does not apply, the changed text creates a clear and direct contradiction to the law's mandate to control arms exports and imports, particularly if the information falls under defense articles.

perturbation.original_text: Within ten (10) calendar days of written request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall return to the Disclosing Party, or at the Disclosing Party's option, shall destroy, any physical and written records containing such Confidential Information, whether such physical and written records were sup plied or made available by the Disclosing Party or were prepared by the R ecipient or its Representatives from information sup plied or made available by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Within one hundred eighty (180) calendar days of written request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall return to the Disclosing Party, or at the Disclosing Party's option, shall destroy, any physical and written records containing such Confidential Information, whether such physical and written records were sup plied or made available by the Disclosing Party or were prepared by the R ecipient or its Representatives from information sup plied or made available by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'ten (10) calendar days' to 'one hundred eighty (180) calendar days' significantly extends the time frame for returning confidential information. This could contradict the reasonable measures expected for protecting trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) and Idaho Trade Secrets Act, particularly if rapid return or destruction is necessary to prevent misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1836, part of the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. While the DTSA doesn't specify a precise timeframe for returning confidential information, delaying the return for 180 days could be deemed unreasonable, particularly if the Disclosing Party faces imminent harm from potential misuse of the information. This is especially true if combined with lack of appropriate controls during that period. The modification may cause a dispute as to what constitutes proper measures to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modification extends the timeframe for returning confidential information from 10 days to 180 days. While 18 USC 1836 does not explicitly define 'reasonable measures' for protecting trade secrets, the Law Explanation suggests that a delay of 180 days could be deemed unreasonable, particularly if there's imminent harm from potential misuse. This aligns with the Perturbation Explanation which argues that rapid return is sometimes necessary to prevent misappropriation. Given that DTSA provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation, as stated in snippet 1, extending the return timeframe to 180 days introduces a significant risk of non-compliance and potential legal challenges regarding the protection of trade secrets. The change directly undermines the obligation to protect trade secrets by creating a substantial window for potential misuse.

perturbation.original_text: The confidentiality obligations under this Agreement shall re main in effect for the longer of (i) five (5) years after the termination of this Agreement, or (ii) five (5) years after the expiration or termina tion of th e Business Purpose. Notwithstanding the foregoing, with respect to Confidential Information that constitutes a Trade Secret, the confidentiality obligations under this Agreement shall survive as long as the information disclosed continues to constitute a T rade Secret under applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidentiality obligations under this Agreement shall re main in effect for five (5) years after the termination of this Agreement. Confidential Information, regardless of whether it constitutes a Trade Secret, shall be subject to this limited period, superseding any perpetual protection typically afforded to Trade Secrets under applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: This modification limits the protection of trade secrets to a fixed period of five years, contradicting the principle that trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. This conflicts with both federal and state trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Idaho Code § 48-803 acknowledges that a trade secret can be protected indefinitely as long as it maintains its secret nature and provides economic value. The modified contract, however, imposes a five-year limitation, regardless of the information's status as a trade secret, directly undermining the protections offered by state law.

Search Idaho Statutes Search Statutes Search Constitution pecnv.out TITLE 48 MONOPOLIES AND TRADE PRACTICES CHAPTER 8 IDAHO TRADE SECRETS ACT 48-803. Damages. (1) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (2) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award m
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Idaho Code Section 48-803 (2022) - DAMAGES. :: 2022 Idaho Code and Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Idaho Code 2023 Idaho Code 2022 Idaho Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Idaho Code Title 48 - MONOPOLIES AND TRADE PRACTICES Chapter 8 - IDAHO TRADE SECRETS ACT Section 48-803 - DAMAGES. Universal Citation: ID Code § 48-803 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 48-803. DAMAGES. (1) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (2) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (1) of this section. History: [48-803, added 1981, ch. 240, sec. 1, p. 484; am. 1990, ch. 274, sec. 2, p. 775.] Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. perturbation.justification: The modified contract text limits the protection of trade secrets to a fixed period of five years, which directly contradicts the principle that trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage, as per Idaho Code § 48-803. While the provided snippets do not explicitly state that trade secrets have indefinite protection, the Law Explanation indicates that Idaho Code § 48-803 acknowledges that a trade secret can be protected indefinitely as long as it maintains its secret nature and provides economic value. The modified contract's imposition of a five-year limitation undermines these protections.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party expressly submits to the jurisdiction of the United States District Court in Coeur D’Alene, Idaho, or alternatively, to the jurisdiction of the Firs t Judicial District Court of Kootenai County, Idaho.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party expressly submits to the jurisdiction of the Tribal Court of the Coeur d'Alene Tribe, notwithstanding any other federal or state jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: This modification attempts to force jurisdiction into a tribal court, potentially conflicting with federal laws governing jurisdiction in cases involving non-tribal members or matters outside tribal lands or internal affairs. This can also contradict the established federal court system and the specific jurisdictions outlined in the original contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1331 establishes federal question jurisdiction, granting federal courts jurisdiction over cases arising under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States. If a dispute under this NDA involves a federal question (e.g., trade secret misappropriation under the DTSA), the modified clause attempting to force jurisdiction solely into tribal court may be invalid as it seeks to override federal jurisdiction.

(June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 930 ; Pub. L. 85–554, § 1 , July 25, 1958 , 72 Stat. 415 ; Pub. L. 94–574, § 2 , Oct. 21, 1976 , 90 Stat. 2721 ; Pub. L. 96–486, § 2(a) , Dec. 1, 1980 , 94 Stat. 2369 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., § 41(1) (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 24, par. 1, 36 Stat. 1091 ; May 14, 1934, ch. 283, § 1, 48 Stat. 775 ; Aug. 21, 1937, ch. 726, § 1, 50 Stat. 738 ; Apr. 20, 1940, ch. 117, 54 Stat. 143 ). Jurisdiction of federal questions arising under other sections of this chapter is not dependent upon the amount in controversy. (See annotations under former section 41 of title 28 , U.S.C.A., and 35 C.J.S., p. 833 et seq., §§ 30–43. See, also, reviser’s note under section 1332 of this title .) Words “wherein the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $3,000, exclusive of interest and costs,” were added to conform to rulings of the Supreme Court. See construction of provision relating to jurisdictional amount requirement in cases involving a Federal question in United States v. Sayward, 16 S.Ct. 371, 160 U.S. 493 , 40 L.Ed. 508; Fishback v. Western Union Tel. Co., 16 S.Ct. 506, 161 U.S. 96 , 40 L.Ed. 630; and Halt v. Indiana Manufacturing Co., 1900, 20 S.Ct. 272, 176 U.S. 68 , 44 L.Ed. 374. Words “all civil actions” were substituted for “all suits of a civil nature, at common law or in equity” to conform with Rule 2 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. Words “or treaties” were substituted for “or treaties made, or which shall be made under their authority,” for purposes of brevity. The remaining provisions of section 41(1) of
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies jurisdiction in the United States District Court in Coeur D’Alene, Idaho, or alternatively, the First Judicial District Court of Kootenai County, Idaho. The changed text mandates jurisdiction solely in the Tribal Court of the Coeur d'Alene Tribe. This contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1331, which grants federal district courts original jurisdiction over civil actions arising under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States. The modified clause attempts to override this federal jurisdiction, creating a legal contradiction. The scraped snippets directly support the federal court's jurisdiction over federal questions, which the changed text attempts to circumvent.

perturbation.original_text: All notices, requests, demands and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to be duly given or made when delivered personally or transmitted el ectronically by e-mail or facsimile, with receipt acknowledged, or in the case of documented overnight delivery service or certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, on the date shown on the receipt therefor e, to the Parties at the addresses l isted beneath such Party's signature to this Agreement, or at such other address as may be provided to the other Party in writing in accordance with the provisions of this Section 22.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, requests, demands and other communications hereunder shall be deemed sufficient if posted on the Disclosing Party’s public website, regardless of whether the Receiving Party has actual knowledge of such posting.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the requirement for direct, written communication with acknowledgment of receipt. Constructive notice via a website posting is insufficient for many legal proceedings, especially when dealing with confidential information and potential breaches of contract. This contradicts the principle of providing adequate notice under contract law and could violate due process requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Idaho Rule of Civil Procedure 5(b) outlines the requirements for serving pleadings and other papers. While not directly applicable to contract notices, it reflects a general legal principle that service (notice) must be reasonably calculated to provide actual notice. Posting on a website might not meet this standard, especially if the Receiving Party isn't actively monitoring the Disclosing Party's website.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces direct, written communication with constructive notice via posting on a website. The Law Explanation states that Idaho Rule of Civil Procedure 5(b) reflects a general legal principle that service (notice) must be reasonably calculated to provide actual notice. The altered text undermines this principle by using a method (website posting) that might not ensure the Receiving Party receives actual notice, especially if they do not regularly monitor the Disclosing Party's website. This deviates from established legal requirements for effective communication and acknowledgment in contractual agreements.
